sentences.text: I was born December 15, 1922 in Cracow, Poland.
I be-- I am 73.
My father's name was Jakob.
My mother's name was Gusta.
My sister's name was Miriam.
Then after Miriam was Bernard.
Bernard was the youngest.
And my oldest brother was Sam.
My father was born in Dombrowa near Tarnow.
[BACKGROUND NOISE] And he was an orphan.
I-- I don't remember anything about his parents.
But he told me that he was an orphan.
And he has a sister in Dombrowa and a brother.
My father-- this story has to be told from the beginning.
My father came after the Second World War-- after, I'm sorry, after the first World War-- came seeking his-- when he was discharged from the Austrian Army, he was seeking his fortune.
He came to the big city, to Cracow.
And I think he was lodging with my grandmother, who was a widow at that time.
And he was renting out rooms to single people.
And I was told that he fell in love with my mother, who was one of the daughters of-- of my grandmother, of course.
And-- and he didn't have any-- any profession to make a living.
He wanted to marry my mother.
And so my uncle was a shoemaker.
And he taught him the profession of becoming a shoemaker, which I know he didn't like very much.
And he became a shoemaker, and he married my mother.
Oh, I assume this was 1919.
I have the documentation, but I don't know offhand the exact record.
I have the marriage certificate and the birth certificate of my mother and father.
No, he did not remain a shoemaker.
Later on, he became a traveling salesman.
He traveled.
He was-- he was selling billiard pool tables, billiard balls, and cues, and whatever.
He traveled to the countryside carrying his wares on his back and doing a lot of walking, a lot of thumb-- thumb-pointing.
And-- but he always managed to come home for-- when we were children for-- for the Sabbath.
My mother-- my mother had-- let me see how many sisters she had.
She has Ida was the youngest.
Then Haila was the second, then Beyla.
Then Gusta was mother, Gusta.
And then she had a brother, Joel.
So she had four sisters and-- and one brother.
And I don't recall my grandfather because he died before I was born.
And my mother was a dressmaker by profession.
She was-- people used to bring their fabrics, and she used to cut it and make dresses up for them.
[SIGHS] Well, life was-- it wasn't easy.
It was tough.
And we were poor, but [LAUGHS] we didn't know about it because all our friends, all our neighbors were poor too.
So we all were struggling.
But we didn't know.
This was accepted.
We accepted this because we didn't know any better.
Well, life was-- I attended the public school, [NON-ENGLISH].
And-- and I enjoyed playing soccer.
And then we had hike and see-- hide and seek, children's games, all kinds of children's game.
We were happy, very happy.
We used to go to the park play.
We used to play a game called palestra.
This is similar to baseball, but you played with a broomstick and a little piece of about 7, 8 inches thick.
You put it on an angle, and you hit it.
And then you mark off the yardage-- I mean, the meters, how many meters you supposed to.
And this was a game.
We enjoyed playing that.
And we use to break a lot of windows on the streets.
And we used to have to pay the price.
Whoever we broke a window came down running and grabbed one of our hats or our jacket, whatever.
Father had to bail it out afterwards and pay for the window.
But this was, you know, like young kids.
But soccer was the main-- my main interest.
I used to love playing soccer.
Well, we were not considered as a European standard.
We were not considered European standard.
We were not all Orthodox.
But here, we would be considered Orthodox because we were observant.
Father never worked on Shabbat.
He went to synagogue.
And we had to go to the synagogue.
And we didn't cook or bake on-- on-- on Shabbat.
We didn't shop.
So we-- we were observant.
But according to the European standard, we were not Orthodox enough.

Oh, used to enjoy all the holidays because, you know, you used to get the new clothing for the holidays, like kids go to the synagogue and show off your new jacket or your new shoes, you know, to the other kids.
It was fun.
And in the meantime, during the synagogue, we used to like to run out like kids and play a little soccer.
And father limited us.
He used to come out and get us.
Come in back to the synagogue because we stayed out too long.
It was-- it was fun.
It was fun.
And the preparation, mother was baking and cooking for everything was.
We didn't buy any cakes.
Mother was baking the-- the challahs and the cakes for the Shabbat.
Then we prepare a meal, like for Sabbath that we had to take to the bakery, called the cholent in a big pot.
There was derma.
There were potatoes.
There was kasha.
There were all kinds of piece of flanken.
And we used to take it down to the bakery Friday evening.
And the baker used to put it-- gave us a number, glued on a number.
It was the pot was covered with brown paper with a rope.
That this was the top and on the baker put down the number on this, glue down the number of it and gave us a receipt with our number.
And-- and this was from baking all day Friday, the challahs and the-- and the-- and the cakes, this was-- the stove was hot.
And this was simmering all night and baking on that very low, low heat.
And this was.
And after the synagogue on Saturday, we used to pick it up and take it home.
And this was our main meal on Saturday since we-- mother didn't cook or didn't make-- light the fire or anything.
Well, I attended Polish schools.
And it's supposed to be-- it's supposed to be a public school.
And they're supposed to be in a democratic country.
But it was only in letters.
It wasn't, because in school was the pictures in front of the teacher.
In the background of the teacher of her table there was the President and a cross, and was Jesus on the cross.
This was a public school.
So it wasn't-- But we had-- the only thing that we had when the Christian children had their religion with the Priest, we had a-- a professor of-- from the Hebrew school.
We had that professor teaching the religion.
I don't remember the hours when it was, but it was maybe once a week or so for.
But then in the after school-- I think the school was till 1 or 2 o'clock-- then we went to Hebrew school in the afternoon on our own.
No, we played soccer, but not-- not personal.
They didn't want to be friends with us, and the same thing was our parents didn't care that we'd be friends with them.
So it was a mixed bag, so to speak.
Well, I was-- I was good with my hands.
I was very mechanically inclined.
I was fixing things.
I was doing-- I was building crystal sets.
I enjoyed that type of work.
I was always fixing something for the neighbor, always manage something to get a few pennies.
Pennies were [POLISH], called in Polish.
And, you know, I always had for a few.
We didn't get any spending money or anything.
There was no toy store that you went out.
You make your own toys.

And your biggest toy was a old carriage with four wheels if you could manage get it from some yard somewhere, buy it or something and riding down the hill, or creating your own toys, whatever you could make with your hands, put together.
This was the toys that was.
But life, life wasn't bad, wasn't bad.
Was very disciplined.
School was very disciplined.
The teachers were-- some of the teachers-- one in particular teacher was very sadistic.
And if you didn't obey something, he'd call you in front of him and you had to raise your hand.
And he used to take you-- that hand is too high.
Now it's too high.
It's too high.
Lower it down a little bit.
Lower it down a little bit.
Then he slap you with the-- with the ruler right in your hand.
Must have been 12.
I got odd job.
I have a job working with a plumber, which they didn't teach me anything.
They just use me as a horse.
They put on a lot of-- on the wagon.
They put in a lot of toilets and pipes and so forth.
I had to pull it, put like a harness on my-- on my shoulder and pull it up, up, up on cobblestone street-- roads and so forth.
And they supposed to teach me something, but they never did.
And I quit.
I came home.
I was crying.
And I quit the job.
Then I got a job as a-- working in a dry goods store-- in a dry goods store.
And I think the war broke out.
This was already 1939.
Oh, we were aware of it because I think it was in 1939 my father's uncle, I think, was-- was-- was thrown out of Germany because he was born in Poland.
Hitler was in power.
And we knew about the Anschluss in Austria and-- and-- and marching in the Sudetenland.
And we knew what was coming.
But there was no-- nothing that we could have done about it.
Yes, there was a lot of-- the lot of Zionist organization that they were-- they were training.
They were working in the countryside, learning to farm.
And there was a [NON-ENGLISH].
[NON-ENGLISH] means that they were studying farming and so forth, being away from home.
And they used to go illegally to Israel because it was already prohibited by the British, the 'White Paper,' whatever you call it.
No.
No.
Just before the war broke out?
Well, father was traveling a lot, being a lot away from home.
Like I said, before, he was used to come home Friday night.
And the kids-- the kids had-- we had a lot of freedom.
We had a lot of freedom.
But we were not mischievous.
We didn't create any problems.
All we were interested is-- is we were busy, you know?
You came-- you came home from school.
Then you had to have something to eat.
And then you had to go to five days, six day to-- to Hebrew school in the evening.
So-- but it was-- it-- When you were working in a dry goods store?
No.
No, I'm-- you asked me a question before.
Yeah.
Oh, no.
This was-- no.
No, no.
I'm going back a few years before-- before-- before the war started.
I'm going back to the 1935, '37, '36, before I worked in a dry goods store.
This was-- and we had to go to Hebrew school.
And it wasn't easy to get to Hebrew school because it was like there was in the back of the Hebrew school was a big hill, a big mountains.
And-- and Christian kids used to throw rocks.
And sometimes, I used to get hurt with the rock they were throwing-- throwing at us when we were going to Hebrew school.
So it was no picnic to get down there.
Yes.
My brother was working already.
My older brother Sam was working already as said farrier.
He was learning to be a farrier.
He worked in Cracow.
And he commute to the city every day, every day.
And he was working.
He had a-- Sam, this was my oldest brother Sam.
That's correct.
No, Bernard was-- Bernard is-- Miriam and then Bernard.
Bernard was the youngest, so Bernard is about six, seven years younger than I.
Yes.
Miriam was also in school.
Well, to the German invasion, it started before when Germany invaded Poland.
It was-- they form a civil defense.
And every building was in charge of their own-- of their own-- had to have guards outside.
And we had to schedule.
People had to schedule sitting guard outside the house, see-- looking that there's no strangers coming.
The lights had to be turned off.
There was no lights.
And-- and I recall on one of those nights, I was 1 o'clock or 2 o'clock in the morning.
It was a dark night, and some stranger came over.
I was sitting in front of the building.
I was scared.
I was holding like civil defense guard.
And he was starting-- asking me question about this and that.
And he was asking me about a-- a particular place where-- where the army was, this was.
And-- and he was holding his hands in his pocket.
And I was pretty scared.
I was pretty scared.
I don't know who he was.
Maybe he was a spy or what.
I don't know.
And that was pretty scary experience.
Then the following morning, they were running, people.
You heard from the distance artillery-- artillery.
And you knew the Germans were coming.
And then a lot of refugees was coming from-- from Silesia, from all that from the German borders.
And one particular family came in.
This was an air raid.
And they came to a shelter in our building.
Was an air air raid, and-- and I helped them to fix an axle on the-- on the what you call it-- on the carriage, on the horse-- horse-drawn carriage.
And I help him.
And he gave me a handful of money.
It was thousands of thousands.
I never saw it in my life so much money.
[LAUGHS] But it was worthless.
He gave a lot of money.
OK.
And a couple days later, this was--
--this was already the-- the--
Yes, it was already.
And the Germans were already advancing.
You heard it already.
And the Polish army decided to open up all the warehouses, all the warehouses in order for the German shouldn't get all the-- all the supplies, food supply.
They opened up the-- the monopole, monopole.
It was the manufacturing of cigarettes.
Cigarettes were only manufactured by the government.
The spirits were also manufacturing by the government only.
The stills were all government owned.
But then they open up flour supply.
[INAUDIBLE], this was called.
And they also opened up the-- the chocolate factories open up.
And they said to the people, go and get it.

So my brother Sam and-- and my little brother Bernard got a baby carriage, and we went and picked up some flour, a sack of flour, and some cigarettes, and we picked up some cigarettes, chocolate, and-- and we brought it home.
So we had a supply of something to eat, because, you see, in Poland, was-- before the war, was like-- you were buying daily supplies.
[LAUGHS] You were not storaging up anything.
There was no refrigeration or anything.
So we were just buying daily food.
So this was something.
Anyhow, the following day, the German marched-- marched in, into Cracow.
This made a tremendous impression on me because we were standing in the doorway, and a German on a tricycle motorbike with a machine gun came into out door, like this, and pointed the gun.
We should go inside.

Anyhow, then we saw men on horses, the SS on horses, on tall horses with their feet practically touching the ground, so big guys, like superior guys, you know, showing that strength, marching through the-- through the-- through the [INAUDIBLE], through the town.
[SIGHS]
And this is a terrific to a kid, made a terrific, psychologically impression, you know, how-- how powerful they are, how strong and big, you know?
But anyhow, then-- then right away, they issue a proclamation.
And then a couple of weeks later or what, in Polish and in German, that all of the Jews have to be identified.
And they cannot do this.
They cannot do that.
And they have to wear armbands.

And especially, they started off with the Jews who they could identify, the Orthodox Jews with the beard, who wore difference black, black hats and black-- they started grabbing them on the street, beating them up, taking pair of scissors and cutting off their beards, and pulling-- ripping out the beards.
And started-- started a lot of trouble.
And then--
Yeah, we had opened the business.
And the Germans-- the business was open.
That guy I worked for on [INAUDIBLE], it was the name was Haber.
He had a dry goods store.
Ha, didn't take long.
The Germans came in and were buying materials, whatever they could.
And they were shipping it to Germany.
And didn't last long.
And this-- the job ended, I think, because they run out of-- of products in the-- of materials, whatever.
And then they used to-- then they used to come with the-- with-- trucks.
They used to grab us on the streets.
And because we wore our armbands and-- and load us on trucks.

And we had to go to the houses, and scrub the floors, and clean everything, and sweep just streets, and all kinds of manual-- manual labor, unloading-- unloading coal from the railroad, and-- and potatoes, whatever-- whatever it was, manual labor.
And we were lucky if I got a bowl of soup for the work.
[SIGHS] And [SIGHS] this was the beginning.
And then was we had to register.
They form a-- they issue another proclamation that all the Jews have to move into a certain section of Cracow.
This is [INAUDIBLE].
And you can bring only so much with you, what you can carry and whatever.
And you have to leave everything.
And--
I lived already in that place where they were forming the ghetto.
So I didn't have to move.
We were already in the ghetto.
So we-- we formed-- what do you call it?
Oh, then-- then we had to start lining up, lining up for bread, to get a loaf of bread.
So I remember on one of those outings standing up all night for a piece of-- the whole family was lining up because just in case they run short of bread, or one of us will be able to get a bread.
So we all line up.
I-- I remember on one of those outings, my mother got bitten by the German dog.
He just-- just got bitten.
And I don't remember even if we got a bread that time.
But we all were lining up all night to get a bread.
And that's what started a lot of-- a lot of trouble, a lot of trouble, a lot of trouble for us. for us.
We were beaten and-- and-- and starved, and-- and then I got a job [SIGHS] going out to a brick factory.
